title: Opportunity Management
description: Master opportunity principles, stages, and the requirement that all sales activity occurs on the Entity Child Account.

sections.title: What Is an Opportunity?
sections.content:
An Opportunity represents a potential sale or commercial deal in Salesforce. It's the hub where qualified leads become tracked pipelines—from first conversation through contract closure.

Opportunities are your window into deal progression, sales velocity, and revenue forecasting. They're the single source of truth for every active deal.

sections.title: The Golden Rule: Entity Child Account Only
sections.content:
All sales activity must occur on the Entity Child Account that will deliver or consume the service—never on the Parent Account.

This is non-negotiable. In complex hierarchies with Parent and Entity Child Accounts, always create opportunities on the business unit that owns the delivery. This keeps revenue attribution accurate and reporting transparent. If an opportunity lands on the wrong account, move it immediately.

sections.title: The Seven Stages
sections.content:
Opportunities flow through seven distinct stages. Each marks a clear shift in the deal's journey.

1. Qualification
Entry point for new deals. You've identified a prospect, confirmed real need, and validated budget and timeline. Record basic details: company, contact, deal name, expected close date.

2. Solution Definition
Deep discovery phase. You're mapping their technical requirements, business processes, and success metrics. You're building a proposed architecture. Multiple stakeholders are engaged—technical discovery calls, whiteboarding sessions, draft proposals. Internal alignment matters here.

3. Pricing & Quotation
Solution is clear; now you price it. You're defining commercial scope—products, services, quantities, SLAs—and preparing formal quotes. Work with product specialists and pricing approvers. Get approval before the customer sees it.

4. Customer Review
Proposal is live. Your customer evaluates, asks questions, requests changes, compares alternatives. Stay engaged. Answer questions. Address concerns. Negotiate internally on pricing and scope as needed.

5. Contracting
Customer accepted the terms. Legal and contracting teams step in. You're drafting binding agreements, securing internal approvals, negotiating final terms, and getting signatures. Multiple rounds are normal.

6. Closed - Won
Contract is signed. Deal is done. Customer is committed. Revenue can be recognized. Document the win reason (solution fit, pricing, POC success) for your win/loss analysis. Transition the account to onboarding.

7. Closed - Lost
Customer went elsewhere, cut budget, or stalled beyond recovery. Document the loss reason (competitor won, misfit, funding issues). Review losses as a team to spot patterns and opportunities to improve.

sections.title: Name Opportunities With Clarity
sections.content:
Opportunity names should tell the story: customer, scope, and value in one line.

Strong names:
- Acme Corp - Financial Services Platform - $500K
- TechCorp - Cloud Migration - Phase 1 - $250K
- GlobalRetail - Inventory System - $1.2M

Avoid vague names like:
- Acme Corp - New Deal
- Big Opportunity
- TBD

sections.title: Essential Fields to Track
sections.content:
- Opportunity Name: Clear, descriptive, customer + scope + value
- Account: Entity Child Account (never Parent)
- Close Date: Expected closure date, updated as deal moves
- Stage: Current phase
- Amount: Contract value
- Probability: Win likelihood aligned to stage (Qualification 20%, Customer Review 70%, Contracting 90%)
- Primary Contact: Lead stakeholder
- Next Steps: Specific action to advance the deal
- Products/Services: What you're selling
- Description: Business context and proposed solution

sections.title: Keep Opportunities Clean
sections.content:
- One deal = one opportunity. Don't bundle expansions, renewals, or different products into one record.
- No speculative deals. Create opportunities only when there's real customer interest.
- Update regularly. Keep Stage, Close Date, and Probability current as the deal moves.
- Document Next Steps. Be clear about what's required to advance.
- Track losses thoroughly. Analyze reasons and document for team learning.
- Prepare for transitions. When you win, hand off to onboarding and account management smoothly.

Clean opportunities are the foundation of accurate forecasting and pipeline health. Leadership relies on them to understand business trajectory.

keyTakeaways: All sales activity must occur on the Entity Child Account only, ensuring correct revenue attribution and clean reporting
Seven stages (Qualification through Closed - Won/Lost) represent distinct phases of the sales process, each requiring specific information
Accurate, disciplined opportunity management is the foundation of sales forecasting and pipeline reporting
